text: Semantic Comparison of Asset Administration Shells Torben Miny∗, Sebastian Heppner∗, Igor Garmaev∗, Marko Ristin¶, Bj¨orn Otto‡, Nico Braunisch§, Tobias Kleinert∗, Hans Wernher van de Venn†, and Martin Wollschlaeger§ ∗ Chair of Information and Automation Systems, RWTH Aachen University, Aachen, Germany {t.miny, s.heppner, i.garmaev, kleinert}@iat.rwth-aachen.de † Institute of Mechatronic Systems, Zurich University of Applied Sciences (ZHAW), Zurich, Switzerland vhns@zhaw.ch ‡ Institute for Automation and Communication, Otto von Guericke University, Magdeburg, Germany bjoern.otto@ifak.eu § Institute of Applied Computer Science, TU Dresden, Dresden, Germany {nico.braunisch, martin.wollschlaeger}@tu-dresden.de ¶ Nazarbayev University, Astana, Kazakhstan marko.ristin@nu.edu.kz Abstract—This paper presents a novel approach for the se- mantic comparison of Asset Administration Shells (AAS), i.e., digital twins within the context of Industry 4.0. As digital twins gain importance in optimizing industrial processes, ensuring interoperability and accurate versioning of AAS becomes critical. We propose a method that focuses on object model-level com- parisons rather than mere syntactic differences, enabling a more meaningful assessment of semantic equivalence. Our findings highlight the challenges associated with traditional comparison methods and suggest a comprehensive framework for enhancing AAS versioning and integrity verification. Index Terms—Asset Administration Shell, Comparison, Nor- malization, Canonicalization, Hashing I. INTRODUCTION In the context of digital transformation, particularly within Industry 4.0, the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) plays a central role in creating digital twins of physical systems. It enables a structured and interoperable representation of infor- mation that is essential to optimize industrial processes. AAS can be serialized in various formats, such as JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) or XML (eXtensible Markup Language) [1]. Although both formats can present identical content, their different structures also give rise to specific challenges. In addition, information that conforms to the specification can vary within a format. This makes the comparison of two AAS models non-trivial. A central challenge is ensuring semantic equivalence of the data, especially when different representations of identical values exist, for situations like: • Relevance Check for AAS Version Updates: When a new version of an AAS is released by the manufacturer, operators need to determine whether the changes affect their operations. • Change Logging for Traceability: In scenarios where AAS modifications must be tracked, e.g., for regula- tory compliance or quality assurance, semantic compar- ison ensures that only semantically relevant changes are logged, avoiding noise from purely syntactic differences. • Concurrent Editing and Merging: When multiple stake- holders edit copies of an AAS in parallel, changes must be detected and visualized for merging (see Figure 1). • Local versus Server Version: Developers frequently com- pare a locally modified AAS with a reference version stored on a server. Fig. 1. Example workflow where semantic comparison is needed. Upon closer examination, establishing semantic equivalence for AAS instances is far from straightforward. For instance, the integer number 2 has multiple representations – “2”, “+2”, “+0002” – each of which is semantically equal. Similarly, the order of objects within an AAS may vary — submodel elements within a submodel may be arranged in different order without affecting their semantic meaning. For example, imag- ine a submodel with two property elements, “temperature” and “pressure”. Whether the “temperature” or the “pressure” element comes first does not affect what temperature or what pressure is indicated. In simple words, the meaning is the same, but the appearance differs. Such differences in representation can lead to confusion, hinder interoperability and versioning. Therefore, it is essential to develop a method that enables the semantic comparison of two serialized AAS instances to ascertain their equivalence. There already exist different solu- tions for comparing text, JSON or XML documents [2]. Such comparisons often focus solely on syntactic differences and do not capture the underlying semantics of the data. One solution is to compare changes at the object model level rather than the text or serialization level, which provides a more accurate and relevant comparison. This approach focuses on the actual content and structure of the data, rather than superficial text or syntactical differences. This capability is crucial for accurately tracking changes that affect the functionality or behavior of assets. However, there are few such tools tailored for AAS [3]– [6], and none operates on semantical level. Fig. 2. Overview of our method for semantical comparison of AAS models For this reason, the aim of this paper is to propose a concept that checks different AAS files for semantic differences. By comparing at the object model level, it is possible to identify semantic differences on a case-by-case basis. The proposed comparison method (Fig. 2) is crucial for effective versioning and updating of AAS, as it identifies inconsistent representa- tions and ensures that changes are interpreted accurately. The rest of the paper is structured as follows. Section II presents the state of the art in comparison of text documents in general and existing publications and tools for AAS com- parison. Section III presents the problems identified in the comparison of AAS models. To solve these problems, our concept is presented in Section IV, with a brief description of a reference implementation in Section V. Section VI dis- cusses the solution and makes recommendations for the further development of the AAS specification in order to simplify the comparison in the future. Section VII concludes with a summary and an outlook. II. STATE OF THE ART A. Asset Administration Shell The concept of the AAS is a cornerstone technology of I4.0, which was introduced in 2016 as part of the German standard DIN SPEC 91345 [7]. The AAS serves as the formalized and standardized representation of a digital twin, thus facilitating the modeling and design of all aspects of an asset within the I4.0 context. An AAS summarizes and organizes all informa- tion that is known about an asset throughout its lifecycle. The term ’asset’ is understood to include both physical products and software applications. The AAS incorporates a well- defined meta-model, which prescribes how to model an asset, delineates the permitted structure and components, and thus ensures interoperability between suppliers, producers, and cus- tomers across different domains and technologies. This meta- model is composed of diverse elements, ranging from simple data elements (e.g., properties and operation definitions) to complex relationships and references [1]. The specification includes about a hundred constraints with varying levels of formalism to ensure consistency, enforce intended semantics, and prevent ambiguous models [8]. The development of the AAS specification has been led by Plattform Industrie 4.0 and subsequently by the Industrial Digital Twin Association e. V. (IDTA), gaining international recognition and incorporation into the IEC 63278 series [9]. The AAS specification is comprised of multiple parts, where Part 1 defines a UML- based meta-model and specifies how it should be serialized to file formats for data exchange, such as JSON, XML, and RDF, Part 2 defines the API-Specification, Part 3 provides extensions for Data Specifications, Part 4 gives guidelines for security, and Part 5 defines another exchange format, the Package File Format (AASX) 1. B. Comparison of Text-Files Comparing plain text text files is a common task in various fields, including software development, academic research, and document editing. There are several methods for comparing plain text text files: a) Manual Comparison: This traditional method in- volves reading and comparing files line-by-line. While it can be thorough, it is time-consuming and prone to human error. Manual comparison is best suited for small files or when a detailed understanding of the content is required [10]. b) Line-by-Line Comparison: This is one of the most common and widely-used methods for comparing text files. Tools like diff on Unix systems or graphical interfaces such as Meld [11] and Diffchecker [12] perform a line-by- line comparison to show additions, deletions, or changes. This approach is particularly effective when the text is organized in a structured, line-oriented manner, such as in source code or configuration files. 1https://industrialdigitaltwin.org/en/content-hub/aasspecifications Accessed: 2025-04-20 c) Character-by-Character Comparison: In this method, the comparison is performed at the character level, allowing for very fine-grained difference detection. It is especially useful when minor changes, such as punctuation or spacing, are important. Tools like TextCompare [13] offer online interfaces for character-based diffs. d) Lexical or Word-Based Comparison: Instead of com- paring lines or characters, this approach splits text into words and compares these tokens. It is commonly used in word processors and document review systems. This method is ideal for prose or documentation where changes might occur within the same line but involve different wording. [14] e) Hash-Based Comparison: Hash functions like MD5 or SHA-256 can be used to compute checksums of entire files or blocks of text. If two files produce different hash values, they are not identical. This method is fast and commonly used in data integrity verification but does not provide information on what the differences are [15]. f) Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) Comparison: Primarily used for source code, AST comparison parses text into structural elements like functions or blocks, then compares their hierarchical representations [16]. Tools such as Clang’s AST diff or Git diff with syntax-aware features support this approach. It is highly effective in understanding structural changes in code rather than formatting differences [17]. g) Diff Algorithms (Myers, Patience): Advanced algo- rithms such as Myers’ and Patience diff are used by version control systems like Git to generate human-readable and minimal-edit diffs. The Patience diff algorithm, in particular, is known for producing cleaner diffs by focusing on unique lines and sequences [18]. h) Normalized comparison: Text normalization involves preprocessing the input files – removing redundant punctu- ation, or stripping whitespace – to ensure that superficial formatting does not affect comparison results. For example, this is commonly used for producing comparable JSON doc- uments [19]. i) Canonical Comparison: Many formats allow for dif- ferent representations of the same values. This is common in structured documents, such as XML, where the format allows for a wide variety of representations to make it easier for the users to enter the values. For example, boolean values can be represented with all “0”, “1”, “false” and “true” in XML documents. However, for comparison, canonical representation capturing the underlying semantical value is used. In the case of XML boolean values, the canonical values are only “false” and “true”. The comparison of structured documents is thus performed on the canonical values of document elements rather on their given representations [20]. j) Levenshtein Distance and Edit Distance: This method computes the number of operations (insertions, deletions, substitutions) required to transform one string into another. It is widely used in spell checking, fuzzy matching, and sim- ilarity scoring. Libraries like textdistance and Python’s difflib module provide implementations of edit distance algorithms [21]. k) Machine Learning-Based Comparison: Machine learning models, especially Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms and transformers like BERT or Word2Vec, can be employed to compare the semantic similarity of two pieces of text. These models convert text into vectors (embeddings) and compare them using cosine similarity. Such approaches are useful in document clustering, duplicate detection, paraphrase identification and recognition, or plagiarism detection [22]. C. Related Work on Comparison and Versioning of AAS As part of this work, we conducted a systematic literature review to identify and analyze existing approaches related to the comparison and versioning of Asset Administration Shells. To this end, we searched Google Scholar using both English and German keywords, including AAS, Asset Administration Shell, VWS, and Verwaltungsschale in combination with Com- parison, Vergleich, Version, Versioning, Versionierung, Hash, Continuous, History, Historian, Difference, Diff and Unter- schied. In [3], the authors propose extending each Submodel within the AAS with a historical data storage mechanism. Among the proposed methods is the maintenance of a modification log for each Submodel, thereby enabling a form of change tracking and rudimentary versioning. In approach [4], the authors employ Git as a version control system for AAS by unpacking AASX files and tracking the resulting JSON or XML representations within a Git repository as plain text. A similar strategy is presented in [5], where Git is also used to manage different versions of the AAS, enabling change tracking and collaborative engineering workflows. Furthermore, [6] introduces a novel Submodel template called “Signature.” This Submodel stores, among other ele- ments, chains of hash values computed over canonical JSON serializations of referenced Submodels. This approach ensures data integrity and can also be used as a foundation for version tracking by comparing hash histories. However, canonical JSON serialization does not equate to canonical AAS. For example, canonical JSON treats integer values ”002” and ”2”, given as JSON strings, as different even tough they are semantically same for an AAS Property/value attribute. These works illustrate various directions in which the re- search community is addressing the challenges of AAS com- parison, version control, and integrity verification. However, a standardized and comprehensive solution that addresses both semantic and structural comparison of AAS models, along with fine-grained versioning, remains an open research area. III. CHALLENGES OF COMPARING AAS Comparing two AAS presents several challenges that must be addressed, including canonicalization of data formats and handling associations. In particular, the two AAS models need to be brought to a canonical form such that only semantical differences are detected. In the following, we identify and elaborate fundamental families of canonicalization problems. A. XSD Data Types Various AAS attributes use XSD 1.0 data types to define their values (e.g., Property, Range, etc.). Simple text comparisons are insufficient, as different string representations can refer to the same semantic value. An integer such as 2 can be represented as xs:integer “002”, “+2”, or “2”. A time duration of one day can appear as xs:duration “P1D”, “PT24H”, or “PT1440M”, etc. Although semantically equivalent, these variants differ syntactically and would be misreported as differences under plain text comparison. The “002” and “+2” are obviously different strings, but represent the same semantic value. B. Ordering of Associations Associations in the AAS meta-model are represented as ordered lists in JSON and XML serializations [23]. However, there are only four ordered association in the meta-model, namely SubmodelElementList/value and inputVariables, outputVariables and inoutputVariables in Operation [1]. For all the other associations such as Qualifiable/qualifiers, Submodel/value or SubmodelElementCollection/value, the order is semantically irrelevant, so a consistent ordering strategy is required to canonicalize them. C. Language Identifiers Some classes in the meta-model feature multi-language strings. These are basically an association of language- translation pairs. Furthermore, it is unclear how to han- dle ambiguous language identifiers like en versus en-us. Semnatically they might or might not be equivalent – both designate a variant of the English language. D. Content Type Classes like Blob and Resource feature the attribute ContentType where it is unclear how to compare generic MIME types with more specific ones. For example, are application/json and application/json utf-8 equivalent? E. References The meta-model relies heavily on references. However, comparing them for semantic equivalence is difficult, or im- possible, since they depend on external referencing systems. A problem is that many reference schemes like IRDI have different, but equal serializations. For example, ECLASS with “-” and IEC61360-CDD with “/” (0173-1#01-ACE548#009 and 0112/2///61360 4#AAA012) F. Fuzzy Equivalence Attributes may also be compared in a fuzzy manner. For example, two thumbnails may be considered equivalent even if compressed using different algorithms. Similarly, distinct references may resolve to the same location. G. Side effects Comparing values for equal semantics might eclipse side effects in systems. For example, though “002” and “2.0E01” are semantically the same, there are side effects in the user interfaces, storage capacity etc. Such side effects are highly application specific – what is a side effect for one system, might be irrelevant for the other. For example, two engineers collaboratively editing the same model might consider chang- ing a property’s appearance from the less readable “2.0E01” to the more readable “2” a version change. However, if the model is used only as input for aggregation (e.g., computing a mean), both “2.0E01” and “2” yield the same result. In this case, versioning should ignore differences in appearance, as recalculation is unnecessary when only formatting changes. IV. CONCEPT PROPOSAL In this section we elaborate how to parse and normalize an AAS model, bring it into a canonical form and calculate the hashes, addressing the problems listed above in more abstract terms, while we describe implementation details in Section V. A. Normalization of AAS AAS models come in many different serializations, where the standard ones include JSON and XML [1]. The two different serialization formats are equivalent by design – a model represented in JSON can just as well be converted to XML, with no noticeable differences for the downstream systems. However, this precludes a direct textual comparison, as obviously comparing a JSON document with an XML docu- ment does not make much sense. Furthermore, even comparing two JSON documents is not much more meaningful: there can be redundant spaces between the tokens, missing properties can be represented either through omissions or null etc. To enable meaningful comparison, models must first be mapped to a unified representation that abstracts away such superficial differences. In theory, one could de-serialize the models and re-serialize them into a normalized form, e.g., minified JSON with all the redundant whitespace removed. The normalized representations could be then used for text comparison. While this normalization does away with minor syntactical differences (such as whitespace), it still pertains many other syntactical differences irrelevant for semantic comparison, as we will elaborate in the next section. For instance, a Property with value “+2” should successfully test for equality with another Property with value “2”. Hence, normalization followed by plain text comparison is insufficient for robust semantic equivalence. Instead, we propose a different normalization based on in- memory objects. The two models are de-serialized using an AAS SDK, e.g., aas-core-works2 or Eclipse BaSyx Python SDK 3, for which we know with high probability that the correctness holds [24]. These SDK objects are detached from the original serialization and form an abstract model 2https://github.com/aas-core-works 3https://github.com/eclipse-basyx/basyx-python-sdk representation, akin to abstract syntax trees in programming languages [25]. Canonicalization is then applied to these in- memory objects as the normalized form of the AAS models. B. Canonicalization While normalization abstracts from serialization-specific details, canonicalization eliminates non-semantic variations, as outlined in Section III. In our case, this primarily involves: 1) canonicalizing primitive values, represented using XSD data types in AAS [1], and 2) reordering elements within associations lacking a prescribed order. 1) Canonicalization of XSD Data Types: The specifica- tion of XSD base data types, XSD 1.0 [20], already pro- vides a canonical form for many types. Specifically, we refer the reader to consult the XSD 1.0 for integer types (xs:int, xs:integer, xs:byte etc.), decimals with arbitrary precision (xs:decimal), booleans (xs:bool), hex and base64 encoded binary data (xs:hexBinary and xs:base64Binary), time types (xs:time, xs:date, xs:gYear etc.). However, it is not complete, so it needs to be extended. We propose to normalize the floating-point numbers with finite precision (xs:float and xs:double) based on the scientific notation, with the capital “E” separating the mantissa and the exponent. The mantissa should contain exactly one digit before the decimal point, and one or more digits after the point. All redundant zeros and plus signs are removed from both the exponent and the mantissa. The zero is nor- malized as “0.0E0”. For example, “+00123.45600” becomes “1.23456E2”. XSD 1.0 does not define a canonical form for xs:duration, but XSD 1.1 [20], which remains com- patible with XSD 1.0, introduces one that we adopt. For xs:anyURI, XSD 1.0 references RFC 2396 [26], which lacks a normalization algorithm; we instead follow RFC 3986 [27], which defines one. We refer the reader to the respective specifications. We note that XSD canonicalizations for xs:date, xs:dateTime, and xs:time are well-defined, but subtle. Since xs:dateTime and xs:time include time-of-day information, they can be normalized to UTC time zone to abstract away the time zone offset. In contrast, xs:date lacks this information, so its time zone offset must be preserved. Likewise, xs:duration can be normalized only up to days, as months and years are calendar-based and depend on the starting point (e.g., 2000-01-01 + P1Y1M = 2001-02-01), i.e., a month is not simply 30 days and a year is not 365 days. The examples of our canoncalizations of XSD primitive data types are given in Table I. 2) Ordering of Associations: Unordered associations are represented as lists in AAS serialization [23] (and usually in SDKs as well [28]). They can be thus canonicalized by sorting with a total ordering defined on associated elements. To that end, we define the following total orderings and sort: • Language strings – by language identifiers (unique by the original RDF specification [1]), • References – first by type (external/modeling), then by key values, similar to reference equality comparisons [1], • Extensions – by name (unique by Constraint AASd- 077 [1]), • Embedded data specifications – first by name, then by the reference to the data specification (see the ordering of references above), • Qualifiers – by type; types of qualifiers are always unique in an association (Constraint AASd-021 [1]), • Specific asset IDs – first by name, then by value, • Submodel elements – by their ID-shorts (unique by Constraint AASd-022 [1]), • Identifiables – by their IDs, which are globally unique [1], and • Reference pairs – by their value IDs; value IDs of refer- ence pairs are references (see the ordering of references above). To canonicalize an AAS model, the canonical orderings are applied by recursively sorting all relevant associations. Fig- ure 3 illustrates such a recursive sorting of an AAS submodel. C. Hashing Canonicalizing AAS models at the object level value-by- value suffices for detecting semantic differences but is often too slow in practice when a large number of models need to be compared. A faster alternative is hashing [29], which com- putes a short, practically unique fingerprint of the serialized model. The compactness of fingerprints allows for much faster comparisons than comparing full models [30]. Consistent serialization is essential, as it determines the hash. We adopt the JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS) [19] as the base format. However, JCS is unclear on encoding characters above the Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP, U+10000 and above), leading to platform-dependent differences (e.g., UTF-16 on Windows/Java/C#, UTF-32 on Linux/Python). To ensure uniformity, we serialize such characters using surrogate pairs. For instance, “\U010000” becomes “\uD800\uDC00”. The AAS meta-model defines default values for various attributes, i.e., values assumed when attributes are unset. AAS JSON serialization distinguishes between set and unset values [23], implying defaults as per the AAS meta-model [1]. Therefore, we propose omitting attributes set to their defaults in the canonical serialization for consistency. V. REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION To demonstrate feasibility, we implemented a hashing tool that first canonicalizes the AAS model, and then computes a hash on its serialization for comparisons. We build on the generative approach of aas-core-codegen [28] for increased correctness. Canonicalization of primitive values (Section IV-B) and ordering of associations (Section IV-B2) were written manually due to their specificity. The recursive canonicalization logic was then generated by applying templates to the parsed AAS meta-model (see [28] for details). For each class attribute, we inspect its type and apply the matching template, enabling compact TABLE I REPRESENTATIVE EXAMPLES OF CANONICALIZATION FOR XSD DATA TYPES AS AAS PRIMITIVE VALUES XSD Data Type Value Canonicalization xs:boolean 0 false 1 true false false true true Integers (xs:integer, xs:int etc.) +0016 16 -00016 -16 xs:decimal +00019.84000 19.84 -000.84000 -0.84 xs:float, xs:double +0019.8400E003 1.984E4 0 0.0E0 -00.12345 -1.2345E-1 xs:duration P00Y00M00DT00H00M00.00S P0D PT1.0001234000S PT1.0001234S P1Y1M500D P1Y1M500D P1Y1M500DT24H P1Y1M501D xs:date 002000-01-01 2000-01-01 002000-01-01+02:00 2000-01-01+02:00 xs:dateTime 2000-01-01T01:02:03-02:30 2000-01-01T03:32:03Z xs:time 01:02:03+02:30 22:32:03Z xs:gYear +01984 1984 xs:gYearMonth 001984-01 1984-01 001984-01+02:00 1984-01+02:00 xs:hexBinary dead c0de DEADC0DE xs:base64Binary Z G 9 n ZG9n xs:anyURI HTTP://A.COM:80/b/C/../c?z=3&y=2 http://a.com/b/c?z=3&y=2 specification: only SubmodelElementList/value, Operation/inputVariables, outputVariables and inoutputVariables, and Reference/keys required manual handling. Canonicalization of numeric XSD values and date-times must rely on string operations due to platform-specific pre- cision issues. For example, Python limits date-times to mil- liseconds, and IEEE 754 parsing of xs:float/xs:double can yield inconsistent results, dependent on the platform [31]. The generator comprises 617 lines of code (LoC), producing 801 LoC of canonicalization code. Notably, manual implemen- tation would have been error-prone and labor-intensive due to the need to manually cover every single AAS meta-model attribute. The implementation was completed in 2 days by an engineer with 15 years of experience and is publicly available4. VI. DISCUSSION Semantic comparison of AAS instances showed to be a difficult task. Established solutions offer only limited appli- cability. For example, line-based diff algorithms, designed for plain text, are ill-suited for structured formats like JSON or XML, commonly used for AAS serialization. Furthermore, AASX – a binary container format – must be unpacked before comparison. As the same AAS can be serialized in multiple 4https://github.com/aas-core-works/aas-versioning3.0 ways, comparison tools must abstract from serialization and detect semantic equivalence across formats. The AAS relies on XSD data types to represent primitive values. However, as our analysis shows, their comparison and canonicalization are non-trivial. Although standards ex- ist [20], the AAS specification neither mandates nor defines a canonicalization method, which will eventually lead to interoperability issues. Beyond XSD types, the complexity of the AAS meta-model introduces further challenges that general-purpose diff tools cannot handle. In particular, set-like associations are serialized as lists, with ordering left to be defined by the implementation. To ensure consistent behavior, the AAS specification should define explicit ordering criterion among implementations. Finally, we recommend that the AAS standard incorporate explicit canonicalization and related measures to ensure con- sistency, simplify semantic comparison, and improve interop- erability. A. Testing Establishing the correctness of canonicalization is critical. Once released, semantic comparison and hashing of AAS are adopted widely across the I4.0 ecosystem. Any flaws in canonicalization become nearly impossible to fix, as changes would invalidate existing version hashes and trigger a global re-hashing effort. Canonicalization is also vital for interoper- a) Original submodel b) Canonicalized submodel Fig. 3. An example of recusrive canoncalization of an AAS submodel (written here in pseudocode). The submodel elements as well as values of the submodel element collections are recursively sorted by the respective ID-shorts. ability. Divergent methods lead to mismatched version hashes for semantically equivalent AAS models, potentially causing grave effects such as unnecessary re-synchronizations of large volumes of digital twin data and service disruptions. Thorough testing is therefore essential. In addition to man- ual test cases, two approaches have proven effective in the AAS context: Property-Based Testing (PBT) [8], [28], [32] and Fuzzing via Symbolic Execution [33]. We recommend both for validating canonicalization, and outline them briefly here while we leave details for future work. PBT is a black-box method where random inputs are generated according to scripted rules. For instance, canonical xs:integer values can be “uncanonicalized” by adding leading zeros or plus signs, then re-canonicalized and checked for equivalence. While PBT improves coverage [24], [32], the generators has to be manually written. Fuzzing via symbolic execution offers a white-box alter- native with less setup [33]. Inputs are symbolic values, and the testing engine tracks them through the system exploring all execution paths to check for vioulation of tester-defined output contracts (e.g., no redundant zeros). Whereas PBT is procedural, symbolic fuzzing is declarative and complemen- tary. We emphasize again that, until thorough testing is com- pleted, semantic comparison of AAS should not be deployed in practice. B. Open Challenges Some challenges in Section III resist strict algorithmic solu- tions and instead require recommended practices. In particular, matching language identifiers, content types, or references often depends on application-specific context. When specifying language identifiers and content types, we recommend using the most general form. For instance, unless application-specific needs dictate otherwise, use en instead of en-us, and prefer application/json over application/json-utf-8. Thus, differing identifiers should be assumed intentional, and not to be dismissed as modeling artifacts. For references with different schemes (e.g., IRDI [34]), we suggest that a standardization body such as IDTA 5 defines the preferred scheme. Canonicalization rules must be rigid, as hashes depend on them. Any change requires all systems to re-hash their AAS models, which is prohibitively computationally costly. In contrast, comparison algorithms can be relaxed to reflect application-specific notions of difference, incorporating fuzzy equivalence (Section III-F) or side effects (Section III-G). Specifically, if an application requires side effects to be con- sidered, we propose omitting canonicalization and comparing models as-are. Models should still be serialized using JCS (Section IV-C), with the secondary side-effect-aware hash computed solely on this normalized, but not canonicalized, serialization form. Finally, many comparison aspects will remain open for practical reasons. For instance, two references may point to the same concept (e.g., different semantic IDs), but resolving all references on every comparison is often infeasible due to excessive network requests and lookups. 5https://industrialdigitaltwin.org VII. CONCLUSION AND OUTLOOK In summary, this paper highlights the urgent need for effec- tive methods for semantic comparison of AAS within Industry 4.0 contexts. As industries increasingly adopt digital twins to optimize operations and enhance efficiency, ensuring that these representations are interoperable and accurately versioned is paramount. Our proposed method focuses on object model- level comparisons, which allows for a more nuanced under- standing of semantic equivalence than traditional syntactic approaches. By systematically identifying and addressing the challenges associated with varying data representations, we have laid the groundwork for a more reliable mechanism for change tracking and versioning in AAS. The implications of our findings extend beyond mere technical improvements; they also contribute to fostering trust among stakeholders in industrial ecosystems by ensuring that asset information remains consistent and interpretable across different contexts. Looking ahead, future research should focus on refining our proposed framework and developing standardized tools that fa- cilitate its implementation. Exploring integration with existing version control systems like Git could enhance collaborative workflows in managing AAS versions. Ultimately, our goal is to contribute to the ongoing development of robust standards for AAS that facilitate seamless information exchange across diverse industrial environments.
